summary: McKenzie reviews ByteCommerce, a digital download service that pitched him via email, and finds its supposedly innovative pricing structure uncompetitive. The service takes every 20th sale as its fee, effectively a 5% commission, which McKenzie calculates is more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month for anyone making over $100/month. He also identifies a practical problem with refunds on sales collected by a third party. The post concludes with broader lessons about why competing on price alone is dangerous without a structural cost advantage.
key_points: ByteCommerce's 'every 20th sale' pricing is effectively a 5% commission, which is more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month for most real businesses
Trying to compete with established firms only on price in software is suicidal, especially when you aren't actually cheaper
Being the cheapest is only a competitive moat if you have a structural advantage that lets you always remain the cheapest
The pricing model creates practical problems like the inability to easily refund charges collected by a third party
money_quotes: Trying to compete with established firms only on price in software is kind of suicidal.
I don't choose business partners based on innovative pricing, I choose them based on cheap pricing, if I consider pricing at all.
"We're the cheapest!" is only a competitive moat to the extent that you have some sort of structural advantage that will let you always remain the cheapest, like Wal-Mart does.
Pricing is not good ground to pick a fight on in selling software.
$5 a month is less than 5% of gross sales for any number of gross sales greater than $100 a month, and thats been me since the second month I was in business.

He at least went to the trouble to get my name right and connect the sales pitch tangentially to my main concern, so I'll charitably overlook the unsolicited nature of the email and comment on the product. Unfortunately, its not going to be a very positive comment.
key_insight: Competing on price only works as a strategy if you have a structural cost advantage that ensures you can always be the cheapest; otherwise, a flat-fee competitor will inevitably undercut any percentage-based pricing model as customers scale.
